text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic proctopexy for full thickness rectal prolapse.
This was a patient who had had prolapse for several years and finally was interested in repair.
A proctopexy is my go-to procedure these days.
This is a patient who otherwise might even be considered for a perineal procedure, just given her age and other comorbidities.
But I have been so happy with the speed and safety of a proctopexy.
That is my go-to for even patients in the past that I used to do an Altmyer.
The surgeon opens the area over the sacral promontory and performs a posterior and right lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.
It can actually be a little bit more challenging in rectal prolapse cases to do this because there's a lot of scarring in the retroperitoneum and the significant redundancy.
But once you see the proper crackly fascia and the smooth glistening of the back of the you can get down there pretty quickly.
I usually do two lefts and one right when I do these robotically.

The surgeon's robotic arm holding scissors is positioned in arm four, occasionally contacting the iliac vessels on the sidewall. To improve the angle for pelvic access, the camera is moved from port three to port two, and the setup is adjusted to use two right arms and one left arm, with the scissors now in port three.
The surgeon is positioned far down posteriorly, performing dissection on the right lateral side. The right lateral stalks are visible, though they are typically preserved during this procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection downwards while maintaining hemostasis, with visible presacral veins appearing engorged and prominent in the surgical field.

We recently had another post talking about the dangers of getting into those vessels and what can happen to the patient. So adhering to the proper planes is really important anytime you're doing this type of dissection.
The camera is adjusted upwards to provide a view of the deep pelvis, where the surgeon lifts the mesorectum off the fascia of the levator muscles.
Prior to my access to the robot, these would always be laparoscopic cases for me. I would do it exactly the same. It would be a three-port laparoscopic procedure.

I just find the control and the reticulation of the instrumentation to just be a lot more comfortable for myself to do these robotically. But if the robot wasn't available on the day that I booked the surgery, then I will do it laparoscopically.

The surgeon approaches anteriorly, navigating around the area where the patient has previously undergone a hysterectomy. The rectovaginal septum is being identified, with the white tissue of the vagina visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses digital palpation from below to confirm being in the proper dissection plane and assesses the depth, deciding to continue the dissection to a lower level.
You always have to be mindful of some vessels in this plane, which I'll get into right here.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection in the plane associated with ventral rectopexy, revealing the anal coccygeal ligament.
The surgeon prepares to perform the pexy, positioning the instruments in the surgical field after completing the dissection around the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon carefully cleans the sacral promontory, removing any visible nerves or vessels from the surface of the bone.
The surgeon takes additional bites of the mesorectum, carefully cleaning the area to manage any bleeding and enhance the security of the tissue.

The surgeon uses robotic arm number one to hold the bowel under tension while tying a knot with arms two and four. Despite inadvertently breaking the suture, the surgeon maintains a secure purchase and sufficient length to complete the knot tying.
The surgeon uses silk suture material to take a second bite, typically performing only two bites during this step of the procedure.
And I almost always only will pexy unilaterally to one side and not on both sides. I believe that that can lead to more of a kink in the recto sigmoid at that level. So I don't do that.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal opening along the anterior and right lateral mesorectum using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, ensuring the small bowel does not drop into the posteriorly dissected space.

These cases are taking me about forty-five minutes in total these days with about thirty minutes of consult time. I'm almost exclusively doing these as outpatient procedures. I've been very happy with it. Again, these cases can absolutely be done laparoscopically if you're comfortable with your suturing and probably the same amount of time.
and I really feel happy and comfortable with this procedure for my prolapse patients. I no longer will do a resection.

Although the Frickman procedure, the resection rectopexy was my standard for many years of my career, I have converted to exclusively doing the pexy. I don't use mesh. I am extremely happy with my results. There are definitely many ways to do it. This is simply mine.
The surgeon inspects the pexy to ensure appropriate tension and checks for any serosal injuries on the bowel, concluding the operation.
